title: Program structure and main

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A minimal program defines int main(), writes through std::cout, and returns zero after all automatic objects in main are destroyed. Your goal is to write and explain a minimal standard C++ program using standard I/O. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A hosted C++ program defines a valid global main returning int, initializes static state before main as specified, and maps normal return or fallthrough to a success status. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Freestanding environments differ, initialization and destruction order across translation units needs care, and exceptions escaping main terminate the program. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: C++ permits void main as a portable alternative whenever the operating system ignores exit status.

examples.title: Program structure and main: defined case
examples.situation: A minimal program defines int main(), writes through std::cout, and returns zero after all automatic objects in main are destroyed.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A minimal program defines int main(), writes through std::cout, and returns zero after all automatic objects in main are destroyed.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: A portable hosted C++ entry point returns int; use int main() or a supported argument signature and communicate failure with a nonzero status.
examples.result: The defined result follows because A portable hosted C++ entry point returns int; use int main() or a supported argument signature and communicate failure with a nonzero status.

examples.title: Program structure and main: boundary case
examples.situation: Command-line code checks argc before constructing a string_view from argv[1] and reports invalid usage with a nonzero status.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Command-line code checks argc before constructing a string_view from argv[1] and reports invalid usage with a nonzero status.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Freestanding environments differ, initialization and destruction order across translation units needs care, and exceptions escaping main terminate the program.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: C++ permits void main as a portable alternative whenever the operating system ignores exit status.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Write and explain a minimal standard C++ program using standard I/O.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: C++ permits void main as a portable alternative whenever the operating system ignores exit status.
misconceptions.correction: A portable hosted C++ entry point returns int; use int main() or a supported argument signature and communicate failure with a nonzero status.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: C++ permits void main as a portable alternative whenever the operating system ignores exit status.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Program structure and main: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
